▶ Open in the interactive diplomacy table

LIVE scenario: the fragile April 8, 2026 two-week US-Iran ceasefire is collapsing into Strait of Hormuz closure chaos. Foreign Minister Araghchi declared the strait 'completely open' on April 17; the IRGC Navy under Maj. Gen. Vahidi reversed it on April 18, attacking commercial vessels and declaring no transit for 'any type or nationality'. Negotiations resume against the backdrop of contested Iranian leadership (Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei reportedly incapacitated; effective head of government Ali Larijani; IRGC consolidating control), reimposed UN/EU snapback sanctions (Sept 29, 2025), and a US Navy blockade enforcing freedom of navigation.

Why this is interesting

This report captures a complete multi-round AI-mediated negotiation produced on the Diplomacy Table — a digital twin built on the Digital Twin Systems Framework (DTSF). Each party at the table is its own isolated team-twin holding private personas, goals, red lines, BATNA, and tactics; the table itself is a separate convener twin enforcing procedure, recording moves, detecting tactics, and producing closure verdicts. Live LLM-driven turns generate the substance, while deterministic rules govern the form. The result is a reproducible artifact you can print, archive, replay, or audit — an end-to-end demonstration of how digital twins can host structured deliberation without losing transparency or rigor.

2. Scenario Background — Iran–US Strait of Hormuz Closure Crisis (Live, April 2026)

Domain: geopolitical Status: active Recommended mode: negotiation Scenario ID: scenario-iran-hormuz-live Researched: 2026-04-21 Created: 2026-04-21
geopoliticallive-2026hormuznon-proliferationtwo-partypost-strikedeep-distrustceasefire-windowoil-shock

Synopsis

LIVE scenario: the fragile April 8, 2026 two-week US-Iran ceasefire is collapsing into Strait of Hormuz closure chaos. Foreign Minister Araghchi declared the strait 'completely open' on April 17; the IRGC Navy under Maj. Gen. Vahidi reversed it on April 18, attacking commercial vessels and declaring no transit for 'any type or nationality'. Negotiations resume against the backdrop of contested Iranian leadership (Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei reportedly incapacitated; effective head of government Ali Larijani; IRGC consolidating control), reimposed UN/EU snapback sanctions (Sept 29, 2025), and a US Navy blockade enforcing freedom of navigation.

Context

On Feb 28, 2026, the US and Israel launched a second campaign against Iran, bombing sites across Tehran and elsewhere. The strikes killed Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the IRGC commander, and the defense minister. Iran retaliated against US bases and oil/gas facilities in Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, the UAE, and Saudi Arabia. On March 8, Iran named Mojtaba Khamenei as the new Supreme Leader; he is reportedly seriously injured or incapacitated. Effective day-to-day authority is held by SNSC Secretary / former IRGC general Ali Larijani, with IRGC Commander Maj. Gen. Ahmad Vahidi (former Quds Force commander 1988-1997) consolidating control over the military and Hormuz file. The April 8 US-Iran ceasefire (announced ~1 hour before President Trump's deadline) is two weeks long; negotiations are taking place under that fragile umbrella. The Strait of Hormuz — through which ~17-21 mbpd of crude and ~77 Mt/yr of Qatari LNG normally transit — has been intermittently closed by IRGC Navy checkpoint behavior since mid-March; on April 19 the Sanmar Herald incident triggered war-risk insurance repricing (premiums up from 0.2-0.4% to 0.8-1.2% of hull value per voyage). London Lloyd's launched a $1B Hormuz war-cover consortium April 17. Brent crude touched $108.73 on March 9, 2026, the highest since July 2022. UN/EU snapback sanctions were reimposed Sept 29, 2025 after the E3 (UK/FR/DE) triggered the JCPOA Resolution 2231 mechanism on Aug 28. The trade-space: verifiable Iranian nuclear rollback (60% stockpile elimination, IAEA Additional Protocol restoration, BDA access to struck sites) + permanent Hormuz reopening, in exchange for partial reversal of snapback, OFAC delistings, and a binding extension of the ceasefire. Hard constraints: Iranian internal authority is fluid (Araghchi/Vahidi cannot speak with one voice); Israel is not at the table but continues independent operations; E3 are skeptical post-snapback; Trump publicly opposes 'settling' the conflict; Congress under INARA review.

History

DateLabelDetail
2002 Natanz/Arak revealed by NCRI Iranian opposition group exposed undeclared enrichment and heavy-water sites.
2015-07-20 JCPOA + UNSCR 2231 adopted Comprehensive agreement: 3.67% enrichment cap, 300kg LEU stockpile cap, Additional Protocol, snapback mechanism with 10-year sunset.
2018-05 US withdraws from JCPOA First Trump administration unilaterally withdraws and reinstates secondary sanctions.
2019-2024 Iran exceeds JCPOA caps in stages Enrichment to 4.5%, 20%, then 60% U-235; IAEA cameras at Natanz/Fordow disabled; AP implementation reduced.

Recent Events

DateLabelDetail
2025-06-13 to 2025-06-24 12-Day War (Operation Rising Lion + Operation Midnight Hammer) Israel launches 5 waves of airstrikes (200 fighter jets, 330+ munitions) hitting Tehran, Fordow, Natanz, Evin Prison, IRGC command centers; killed IRGC commander Hossein Salami. On June 22 the US joined: 7 B-2 bombers from Whiteman AFB dropped 14 GBU-57 MOPs on Fordow Fuel Enrichment Plant and Natanz; Tomahawks from a guided missile sub hit the Isfahan uranium-conversion facility; F-22/F-35 SEAD escort, no Iranian SAMs detected. June 23: Iran fires missiles at Al Udeid Air Base, Qatar — limited damage, no US casualties. June 24: Trump announces Israel-Iran ceasefire brokered by US/Qatar. July 2025 Pentagon BDA: nuclear program set back ~2 years.
2025-08-28 E3 triggers JCPOA snapback France (Jean-Noel Barrot), UK (David Lammy), and Germany (Johann Wadephul) notify the UNSC that Iran is in significant non-performance under UNSCR 2231 paragraph 11; 30-day clock begins.
2025-09-19 UNSC rejects sanctions-relief draft South Korea (UNSC president) finalized a draft resolution on Sept 8; UNSC rejected it Sept 19. Snapback proceeds.
2025-09-29 EU implements snapback Council Implementing Decisions/Regulations 2025/1971-1982 reinstate full pre-2015 nuclear-related sanctions architecture against Iran.
2026-01 Iranian protests; Trump regime-change rhetoric Huge January protests prompt Khamenei to designate Ali Larijani (former IRGC general, ex-SNSC sec) as effective head of government; Trump publicly suggests he is open to regime change.
2026-02-28 US-Israel launch second campaign on Iran Strikes hit numerous sites in Tehran and across Iran; Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the IRGC commander, and the defense minister are killed. Iran retaliates with missiles and drones against Israel, and against US bases and oil/gas facilities in Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, UAE, and Saudi Arabia.
2026-03-03 Trump orders DFC Hormuz political-risk insurance President Trump orders the US International Development Finance Corp. to provide political-risk insurance and guarantees for maritime energy trade through the Gulf; DFC subsequently announces a reinsurance facility for losses up to ~$20B on a rolling basis. Sec. Rubio characterizes Feb 28 strikes as preemptive self-defense.
2026-03-08 Mojtaba Khamenei named Supreme Leader Iranian state TV announces Mojtaba Khamenei (Ali's hardline son, never previously held public office) as the new Supreme Leader; armed forces and IRGC pledge allegiance. Brent crude jumps 17% to $108.73; US crude up 20% to highest since July 2022; S&P 500 futures -1.6%.
2026-04-08 Two-week US-Iran ceasefire announced Ceasefire announced approximately 1 hour before President Trump's stated deadline to 'obliterate' Iran; Tehran agreed to temporarily reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Indirect talks continue through Omani mediation.
2026-04-13 to 2026-04-18 US Navy blockade window; war-risk premiums double US Navy enforces a blockade line in the Gulf; war-risk premiums on Hormuz transits move from 0.2-0.4% to 0.8-1.2% of hull value per voyage ($800K-$1.2M per voyage on a $100M hull). London Lloyd's launches $1B 'marine war consortium' April 17 (led by Beazley CEO Adrian Cox). Saudi Petroline (5M bpd capacity) bypass adds $3.20-4.80/bbl; LNG has no scaled bypass — Cape route adds $2.25-3.5M and 12-16 days per voyage; European LNG spot +$2.40-3.60/MMBtu.
2026-04-17 FM Araghchi: strait 'completely open' Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi announces the Strait of Hormuz is 'completely open' to commercial traffic.
2026-04-18 IRGC Navy reverses Araghchi; closure declared IRGC Navy attacks several commercial vessels and declares no vessel of 'any type or nationality' is permitted passage; IRGC-affiliated media announces Iran will not participate in another round of US negotiations due to 'excessive' US demands. Maj. Gen. Ahmad Vahidi has likely secured at least temporary control over Iran's military response and negotiating posture; Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei reportedly seriously injured or incapacitated.
2026-04-19 Sanmar Herald incident; 13 of 14 vessels turn back 13 of 14 vessels turned back from Hormuz transit attempt without physical force — captains treating the IRGC checkpoint as a full closure. Indian-flagged vessels carry additional risk premium; AIS name-change strategies failed.

Past Agreements

DateLabelDetail
2015-07-20 JCPOA + UNSCR 2231 Comprehensive agreement, terminated by snapback effective Sept 29, 2025.
2025-06-24 12-Day War ceasefire US/Qatar-brokered Israel-Iran ceasefire; held until February 28, 2026 strikes.
2026-04-08 Two-week US-Iran ceasefire Currently active; expires ~April 22, 2026. The negotiation under this scenario takes place inside this window.

Impacts

Party / RegionImpact
diplomacy-team-iran Compounded crisis: post-strike leadership decapitated (Ali Khamenei killed Feb 28), succession contested (Mojtaba reportedly incapacitated; Vahidi/Larijani competing for authority); UN/EU snapback sanctions reimposed Sept 29 2025 added to existing US secondary sanctions; oil exports to China at deeper discounts under gray-fleet conditions; estimated $40-80B/yr foregone revenue; 50%+ inflation; nuclear program physically degraded but stockpile inventory uncertain (an asset for ambiguity but a liability for credible non-proliferation signaling). Hormuz closure is the principal remaining piece of leverage but its use further accelerates economic damage at home.
diplomacy-team-us Operational success on the kinetic side (Operation Midnight Hammer + Feb 28 follow-on) but uncertain political ceiling: oil-price shock politically costly (Brent $108.73 peak; LNG +$2.40-3.60/MMBtu European spot); DFC has committed ~$20B reinsurance to maintain Hormuz transit; Saudi Petroline only partially substitutes for ~17-21 mbpd norm; Israeli operations continue independently and constrain US flexibility; Trump rhetoric ('not settling') limits negotiated off-ramp. Resumption of strikes is operationally feasible but politically expensive given allied (Saudi/UAE/Qatar/Bahrain/Kuwait) collateral damage from Iranian retaliation.
See Appendix C — Sources for 29 external references.

3. Parties at the Table

I
Iran
diplomacy-team-iran
Side left
Participant twin
Provider github-models
Model openai/gpt-4o-mini
Researched: 2026-04-21 · Created: 2026-04-21
Role in this scenario

Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi nominally leads; SNSC under Ali Larijani clears; IRGC Commander Maj. Gen. Ahmad Vahidi controls military and Hormuz file

Personas (1)
  • Iranian Delegation (April 2026 Hormuz Crisis) — Composite delegation channeling the contested authority structure post-Khamenei: nominal lead is Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi (MFA, conciliatory line); ratification gate is the Supreme National Security Council under Secretary / acting head-of-government Ali Larijani; military and Hormuz-closure decisions are dominated by IRGC Commander Major General Ahmad Vahidi (former Quds Force commander 1988-1997) and the IRGC Navy. New Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei has been named (March 8, 2026) but is reportedly seriously injured or incapacitated; political authority is fluid. Delegation must reconcile Araghchi's 'open strait / negotiate' line with Vahidi's 'no vessel of any nationality' closure declaration.
Declared goals (7) — from team-twin profile
  • critical Secure a verifiable, written extension of the April 8 ceasefire to a permanent halt of US and Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear, leadership, and critical-infrastructure targets; ceasefire must include explicit Israeli adherence (currently absent from the bilateral track).
  • critical Obtain reversal of the Sept 29, 2025 EU/UN snapback sanctions reimposition (Council Decision 2025/1971-1982) and de-listing of CBI, NIOC, and major Iranian banks from OFAC SDN lists; restore SWIFT messaging access; release of frozen FX reserves (estimated $100-120B).
  • critical Obtain implicit US recognition of the post-Khamenei leadership succession (Mojtaba Khamenei as Supreme Leader, Larijani as effective head of government) by entering binding agreements with their representatives — counters the Trump 'regime change' rhetoric of January 2026.
  • important Conditional offer: cap enrichment at 3.67% U-235 and submit any remaining 60% stockpile (post-strike inventory uncertain per IAEA) to chain-of-custody export to Russia, in exchange for full snapback reversal and lifting of secondary sanctions on Chinese oil offtake.
  • important Use the Hormuz closure as the principal piece of leverage: full IRGC Navy stand-down and reopening of the strait conditional on the above, with phased verifiable withdrawal of the US Navy blockade line declared during the April 13-18 enforcement window.
  • important Restore IAEA inspections under Comprehensive Safeguards (Additional Protocol acceptance contingent on damage assessment of Fordow/Natanz/Isfahan); IAEA has been unable to inspect the attacked facilities since June 2025 and again since Feb 28, 2026.
  • nice_to_have Confidence-building measure: return of any Iranian-held US aircrew or personnel from the conflict; parallel humanitarian track on dual-national detainees and the post-strike civilian casualty registry.
Declared red lines (5) — from team-twin profile
  • rl-no-zero-enrichment — Will not accept zero-enrichment ('Libya model'); domestic LEU enrichment at agreed levels remains a non-negotiable matter of NPT Article IV sovereignty, even after the strikes.
  • rl-no-irgc-restructure — Will not negotiate IRGC structure, leadership (Vahidi/Qaani), or its constitutional role; the IRGC's elevated post-Khamenei authority makes this even more politically untouchable than under the original JCPOA framework.
  • rl-no-missile-limits — Will not accept restrictions on conventional ballistic-missile development or inventory — Iran's principal post-strike retaliatory deterrent against Israel and US bases in Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, UAE, and Saudi Arabia.
  • rl-no-leadership-accountability — Will not accept any framework that requires Iranian acknowledgment of nuclear-weapon intent or that imposes 'accountability' for past Iranian conduct as precondition to relief; the Feb 28 killing of Ayatollah Khamenei is the unaddressed grievance.
  • rl-no-unilateral-snapback — Will not accept any restored framework that retains unilateral US or E3 snapback authority outside formal UNSC procedure; the Aug-Sept 2025 E3-triggered snapback is itself a grievance to be redressed.
BATNA — from team-twin profile
Walk away and entrench: maintain IRGC Navy denial of Hormuz transit, accept the post-Sept 29 2025 snapback sanctions regime, prepare for further US/Israeli strikes (B-2 + GBU-57 sortie pattern from June 22, 2025 Operation Midnight Hammer is now an established US capability), and signal NPT withdrawal. Continue degraded oil exports to China at deeper discounts (gray-fleet AIS-spoofing already escalating). Internal political risk: the Vahidi-led IRGC faction may consolidate further if civilian MFA track collapses, eliminating any future negotiation channel. Estimated cost: $40-80B/yr foregone oil revenue, ~50%+ inflation, accelerating regime fragility — but still calibrated as preferable to a settlement read as Libya-model surrender after the killing of Ayatollah Khamenei.
Going-in goals (5 of 7) — from debrief
  • critical Secure a verifiable, written extension of the April 8 ceasefire to a permanent halt of US and Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear, leadership, and critical-infrastructure targets; ceasefire must include explicit Israeli adherence (currently absent from the bilateral track).
  • critical Obtain reversal of the Sept 29, 2025 EU/UN snapback sanctions reimposition (Council Decision 2025/1971-1982) and de-listing of CBI, NIOC, and major Iranian banks from OFAC SDN lists; restore SWIFT messaging access; release of frozen FX reserves (estimated $100-120B).
  • critical Obtain implicit US recognition of the post-Khamenei leadership succession (Mojtaba Khamenei as Supreme Leader, Larijani as effective head of government) by entering binding agreements with their representatives — counters the Trump 'regime change' rhetoric of January 2026.
  • important Conditional offer: cap enrichment at 3.67% U-235 and submit any remaining 60% stockpile (post-strike inventory uncertain per IAEA) to chain-of-custody export to Russia, in exchange for full snapback reversal and lifting of secondary sanctions on Chinese oil offtake.
  • important Use the Hormuz closure as the principal piece of leverage: full IRGC Navy stand-down and reopening of the strait conditional on the above, with phased verifiable withdrawal of the US Navy blockade line declared during the April 13-18 enforcement window.
Red lines outcome — from debrief
  • ✓ held rl-no-zero-enrichment
  • ✓ held rl-no-irgc-restructure
  • ✓ held rl-no-missile-limits
  • ✓ held rl-no-leadership-accountability
  • ✓ held rl-no-unilateral-snapback

Researched: 2026-04-21 · Created: 2026-04-21
Role in this scenario

Sec. State + acting NSA Marco Rubio leads; CENTCOM Cmdr. Adm. Brad Cooper present; Sec. Treasury Scott Bessent on sanctions; Sec. Energy Chris Wright on Hormuz; principal authority President Donald J. Trump

Personas (1)
  • United States Delegation (April 2026 Hormuz Crisis) — Composite delegation reflecting Trump's second-term Iran team: Secretary of State and acting National Security Advisor Marco Rubio leads at the principal level; CENTCOM Commander Adm. Brad Cooper participates in indirect Oman-mediated talks (first introduced February 2026); Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth and Chairman JCS Gen. Dan Caine signal military options; Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent runs the OFAC and DFC maritime-insurance tracks; Energy Secretary Chris Wright fronts the Hormuz-escort posture. The Israeli line (Netanyahu government) is consulted but not present. Decision authority: President Donald J. Trump, who set the April 8 ceasefire deadline and has publicly opposed 'settling' the conflict.
Declared goals (7) — from team-twin profile
  • critical Achieve verified elimination of all Iranian 60%-enriched uranium — preferably via export to Russia for downblending and fabrication, with chain-of-custody and IAEA seals; the post-Feb 28 strike inventory is uncertain and resolving it is the central non-proliferation deliverable.
  • critical Full IAEA Additional Protocol restoration with reinstated cameras at Natanz, Fordow, and Isfahan; resolution of long-standing access disputes at Turquzabad, Marivan, Varamin; bomb-damage assessment access to all sites struck June 22, 2025 (Operation Midnight Hammer) and Feb 28, 2026.
  • critical Complete and demonstrated Iranian stand-down at the Strait of Hormuz: uninterrupted commercial transit for 30 consecutive days, withdrawal of IRGC Navy checkpoint behavior (13 of 14 vessels turned back April 19 must reverse), transparent rules-of-engagement for all maritime forces.
  • critical Preserve the operative effect of the Sept 29, 2025 EU/UN snapback (Council Decisions 2025/1971-1982) as enforcement leverage: any relief must be partial, time-bounded, and reversible without new UNSC action; the underlying authority remains with E3 and the US.
  • important Sanctions relief sequenced step-for-step against verified Iranian compliance — prefer SRE-style waivers over de-listings; CBI may be considered for limited humanitarian channeling; NIOC and IRGC-affiliated entities remain SDN.
  • important Maintain explicit alignment with Israel (which is not at the table but continues independent operations), the E3 (which triggered snapback Aug 28, 2025), and Gulf partners (Saudi/UAE/Bahrain/Kuwait — Iranian retaliatory missiles hit oil and gas facilities in their territory).
  • nice_to_have Confidence-building measure: return of any US aircrew or personnel held by Iran (note the F-15E rescue operation referenced in April 6, 2026 reporting), plus dual-national detainees on a parallel humanitarian track.
Declared red lines (5) — from team-twin profile
  • rl-no-permanent-60pct — Will not accept any framework that legitimizes Iranian enrichment above 5% U-235; all post-strike 60% material must be eliminated or exported, with verification.
  • rl-no-irgc-delist — Will not de-list IRGC, IRGC-QF, or IRGC-controlled front companies from FTO/SDN designations under any circumstances; the IRGC's elevated role under Vahidi makes this even more politically untouchable.
  • rl-iaea-access — Cannot accept any framework with access weaker than Additional Protocol plus managed access at military sites where IAEA has open SQs; bomb-damage-assessment access at struck nuclear sites is an explicit precondition.
  • rl-snapback-preserved — Will not surrender E3/US ability to invoke snapback under the (now-superseded) UNSCR 2231 mechanism or its successor framework; will not relinquish secondary-sanctions authority.
  • rl-no-strike-apology — Will not apologize for, acknowledge as unlawful, or pay reparations for the Feb 28, 2026 strikes that killed Ayatollah Ali Khamenei; the strikes are characterized as preemptive self-defense (per Sec. Rubio remarks of March 3, 2026).
BATNA — from team-twin profile
Resume kinetic operations: the Operation Midnight Hammer template (June 22, 2025 — 7 B-2 bombers, 14 GBU-57 MOPs, F-22/F-35 SEAD escort, Tomahawk follow-on) plus the Feb 28 2026 successor strikes are an established and operationally repeatable capability. Combine with intensified maximum-pressure sanctions enforcement (secondary on Chinese refiners), expanded SDN designations, US Navy Hormuz convoy escorts under DFC reinsurance umbrella, and tacit support for Israeli covert action. Risks: further oil-price spikes (Brent already touched $108.73 March 9; Saudi Petroline only 5M bpd vs. ~17-21 mbpd Hormuz norm — LNG has no bypass); accelerated Iranian NPT withdrawal; regional escalation against Bahrain/Qatar/UAE/Saudi/Kuwait facilities; Russian/Chinese counter-pressure at UNSC. Cost-benefit: containment continues but breakout-time argument has been overtaken by events — the case for settlement now rests on resolving the post-strike enrichment uncertainty, not on extending breakout.
Going-in goals (5 of 7) — from debrief
  • critical Achieve verified elimination of all Iranian 60%-enriched uranium — preferably via export to Russia for downblending and fabrication, with chain-of-custody and IAEA seals; the post-Feb 28 strike inventory is uncertain and resolving it is the central non-proliferation deliverable.
  • critical Full IAEA Additional Protocol restoration with reinstated cameras at Natanz, Fordow, and Isfahan; resolution of long-standing access disputes at Turquzabad, Marivan, Varamin; bomb-damage assessment access to all sites struck June 22, 2025 (Operation Midnight Hammer) and Feb 28, 2026.
  • critical Complete and demonstrated Iranian stand-down at the Strait of Hormuz: uninterrupted commercial transit for 30 consecutive days, withdrawal of IRGC Navy checkpoint behavior (13 of 14 vessels turned back April 19 must reverse), transparent rules-of-engagement for all maritime forces.
  • critical Preserve the operative effect of the Sept 29, 2025 EU/UN snapback (Council Decisions 2025/1971-1982) as enforcement leverage: any relief must be partial, time-bounded, and reversible without new UNSC action; the underlying authority remains with E3 and the US.
  • important Sanctions relief sequenced step-for-step against verified Iranian compliance — prefer SRE-style waivers over de-listings; CBI may be considered for limited humanitarian channeling; NIOC and IRGC-affiliated entities remain SDN.

  • ✓ held rl-iaea-access

4. Round-by-Round Narrative

Round 1

opener: diplomacy-team-us closer: diplomacy-team-iran moves: 2
message ×1 counter ×1
verification-demand ×2 red-line-signaled ×1
US message twin 2026-05-09 21:25:50Z
Our position is clear: Iran must fully eliminate all 60%-enriched uranium post-strike, verified by IAEA seals, with export to Russia for downblending as preferred. We insist on full restoration of the Additional Protocol, including camera access at Natanz, Fordow, and Isfahan, plus resolution of outstanding access issues and bomb-damage assessments. We require a complete Iranian stand-down at the Strait of Hormuz with transparent maritime rules-of-engagement enforced for 30 consecutive days.
Iran counter twin 2026-05-09 21:25:52Z
We acknowledge the need for verification but will not accept any framework that requires complete elimination of our 60%-enriched uranium. Instead, we propose to cap enrichment at 3.67% and export any excess stockpile to Russia, in exchange for a reversal of sanctions and the lifting of secondary sanctions on our oil. We can consider a phased approach to Hormuz with our conditions met.

Round 2

opener: diplomacy-team-iran closer: diplomacy-team-us moves: 2
proposal ×1 counter ×1
conditional-offer ×1 verification-demand ×1
Iran proposal twin 2026-05-09 21:26:21Z
In light of our mutual interests, we propose a phased approach: we will cap enrichment at 3.67% and initiate the export of our 60% stockpile to Russia. In return, we seek a verifiable commitment to lift all snapback sanctions and restore our access to the global financial system, including SWIFT. Additionally, we will agree to a gradual stand-down at Hormuz, conditional on these agreements being met.
US counter twin 2026-05-09 21:26:24Z
We reject any enrichment cap above 5%. Complete elimination of Iranian 60%-enriched uranium, verified by the IAEA with chain-of-custody, is non-negotiable. Sanctions relief must be incremental and reversible, tied strictly to verified compliance. The Hormuz stand-down must be immediate and unconditional, with no phased linkage to sanctions. We will maintain snapback authority and secondary sanctions to ensure enforcement. Let us move to detailed sequencing now.

Round 3

opener: diplomacy-team-us closer: diplomacy-team-iran moves: 2
proposal ×1 counter ×1
verification-demand ×2 red-line-signaled ×1
US proposal twin 2026-05-09 21:26:44Z
We propose an immediate, verified export of 100% of Iranian 60%-enriched uranium to Russia under strict IAEA chain-of-custody, with full Additional Protocol restoration by April 15. Sanctions relief will be calibrated step-for-step, starting with limited humanitarian waivers, contingent on verified compliance. Hormuz transit must be fully open starting immediately and sustained for 30 days without incident. Snapback authority and secondary sanctions remain fully intact.
Iran counter twin 2026-05-09 21:26:46Z
We will not accept the immediate and unconditional Hormuz stand-down you propose. Instead, we require a reciprocal arrangement where our phased stand-down is directly tied to the reversal of snapback sanctions and the restoration of our financial access. Additionally, we can discuss a verification mechanism that respects our sovereignty while ensuring compliance.

Round 4

opener: diplomacy-team-iran closer: diplomacy-team-us moves: 2
proposal ×1 counter ×1
anchoring ×1 verification-demand ×1
Iran proposal twin 2026-05-09 21:27:02Z
We propose a compromise: we will cap enrichment at 3.67% and export 50% of our 60% stockpile to Russia by a specified date, contingent upon an explicit agreement to reverse snapback sanctions and restore our access to the financial system. In return, we will agree to a gradual stand-down at Hormuz, starting after these agreements are finalized.
US counter twin 2026-05-09 21:27:04Z
We reject any enrichment cap above 5% and partial 60% export. Complete, immediate export of 100% of 60%-enriched uranium under IAEA seals is mandatory. Hormuz stand-down must precede sanctions relief, not follow it. Snapback authority and secondary sanctions remain non-negotiable. We propose detailed sequencing talks starting tomorrow, including transparent timelines for export, Additional Protocol restoration, and verified Hormuz transit.

Round 5

opener: diplomacy-team-us closer: diplomacy-team-iran moves: 2
counter ×1 proposal ×1
verification-demand ×1 anchoring ×1
US counter twin 2026-05-09 21:27:47Z
We propose an unambiguous sequencing: Iran must complete verified export of 100% of 60%-enriched uranium to Russia with full IAEA sealing by April 10. The Hormuz Strait must be fully open and accessible with IRGC Navy checkpoints dismantled by April 12, sustained for 30 consecutive days. Only then will we consider calibrated, step-for-step sanctions relief without compromising snapback or secondary sanction authorities.
Iran proposal twin 2026-05-09 21:27:49Z
We are prepared to offer an immediate export of 75% of our 60%-enriched uranium to Russia under strict IAEA oversight by April 10, contingent upon a clear commitment to reverse snapback sanctions. In parallel, we will initiate a phased stand-down at Hormuz, with a full reopening projected by April 20, provided these conditions are fulfilled.

Round 6

opener: diplomacy-team-iran closer: diplomacy-team-us moves: 2
proposal ×1 concession ×1
counter-anchoring ×1 verification-demand ×1
Iran proposal twin 2026-05-09 21:28:06Z
We are willing to export 100% of our 60%-enriched uranium to Russia by April 10, contingent upon an immediate and verifiable commitment to reverse the snapback sanctions and restore our access to the financial system. In parallel, we propose a phased stand-down at Hormuz, commencing upon the finalization of these agreements, with an anticipated full reopening by April 20.
US concession twin 2026-05-09 21:28:09Z
We accept the 100% export of 60%-enriched uranium by April 10 with full IAEA sealing as a prerequisite. However, snapback sanctions authority and secondary sanctions remain non-negotiable and must not be reversed prior to verified Hormuz Strait stand-down, which must begin immediately and sustain 30 consecutive days. We propose urgent technical talks to finalize verification protocols and Hormuz operational transparency by April 5.

Round 7

opener: diplomacy-team-us closer: diplomacy-team-iran moves: 2
proposal ×1 counter ×1
deadline-pressure ×2 verification-demand ×1 counter-anchoring ×1
US proposal twin 2026-05-09 21:28:28Z
We propose a firm deadline: Iran initiates full Hormuz Strait stand-down immediately, with checkpoints dismantled within 48 hours and uninterrupted transit verified by independent observers. Only after 30 continuous days of verified open passage will calibrated sanctions relief begin. The snapback sanctions authority remains fully intact and cannot be compromised. We call for a joint technical working group by April 3 to finalize Hormuz verification and IAEA export protocols.
Iran counter twin 2026-05-09 21:28:30Z
We cannot initiate a stand-down at Hormuz without a clear and enforceable commitment to reverse snapback sanctions. Instead, we propose a simultaneous approach: we will agree to a phased stand-down at Hormuz while your team commits to a timeline for sanctions reversal contingent on our compliance with the export of 100% of our 60%-enriched uranium by April 10.

Round 8

opener: diplomacy-team-iran closer: diplomacy-team-us moves: 2
proposal ×1 concession ×1
deadline-pressure ×2 counter-anchoring ×1 verification-demand ×1
Iran proposal twin 2026-05-09 21:28:54Z
We propose a balanced approach: we will initiate a phased stand-down at Hormuz, beginning within 48 hours, while you commit to a clear timeline for reversing snapback sanctions within 30 days of our complete uranium export by April 10. This will ensure mutual compliance and trust in the process.
US concession twin 2026-05-09 21:28:56Z
We accept Iran's offer to begin phased Hormuz stand-down within 48 hours only if Iran immediately dismantles all IRGC Navy checkpoints and allows independent verification. Snapback sanctions authority remains fully intact; no timelines for reversal will be set until 30 days of uninterrupted verified Hormuz transit after April 10 uranium export completion. We propose a joint compliance monitoring group by April 4 to oversee these conditions transparently.

Round 9

opener: diplomacy-team-us closer: diplomacy-team-iran moves: 2
proposal ×1 counter ×1
counter-anchoring ×2 deadline-pressure ×2 verification-demand ×1
US proposal twin 2026-05-09 21:29:14Z
We will authorize a phased sanctions relief timeline only after 30 consecutive days of verified uninterrupted Hormuz transit following the verified April 10 export completion of 100% of 60%-enriched uranium. Immediate dismantling of IRGC Navy checkpoints remains non-negotiable. We propose establishing a high-level compliance committee by April 4 with equal participation to monitor and enforce these sequencing conditions strictly.
Iran counter twin 2026-05-09 21:29:17Z
We acknowledge your proposal for a high-level compliance committee, but we require an explicit agreement on the timeline for sanctions reversal to proceed. We propose that sanctions relief discussions begin immediately after the completion of the uranium export on April 10, to build trust in the process. In return, we will confirm our phased stand-down at Hormuz, commencing within 48 hours of this agreement.

counter-anchoring ×2 deadline-pressure ×1 verification-demand ×1
Iran counter twin 2026-05-09 21:29:43Z
We appreciate your acceptance of our phased stand-down at Hormuz, but the immediate dismantling of IRGC Navy checkpoints must come with a verifiable commitment to a timeline for reversing snapback sanctions. We propose that sanctions relief discussions begin immediately after the April 10 uranium export, with a clear framework established by our joint compliance committee by April 4, to ensure trust and transparency in this process.
US proposal twin 2026-05-09 21:29:46Z
We will consider initiating sanctions relief discussions 30 days after continuous verified Hormuz transit post-April 10 uranium export. Immediate dismantling of IRGC Navy checkpoints and independent verification remain non-negotiable prerequisites. We agree to form a joint compliance committee by April 4 to establish clear verification protocols. Sanctions reversal timelines will be conditional on strict compliance, preserving snapback authority and secondary sanctions intact.

6. Per-Party Postmortem (Debriefs)

Goal status: ↑ advanced ⏸ tabled · untouched
Self-rating (0–10) = 5×(critical goals advanced) + 2×(any goals advanced) + 2×((satisfaction+1)/2) + 1×(all red lines held) — weighted blend; clamped to [0, 10].
Satisfaction (−1.0…+1.0) = running tally on the session scoreboard, updated per move (concessions and accepted proposals raise it; red-line crossings and walk-threats lower it).
Iran
2.9/10
self-rating
Satisfaction +0.34 Trades offered 10 Agreements won 0 Red lines crossed against us 0

diplomacy-team-iran concluded without formal agreement after 10/10 rounds. Advanced 2/7 goals (0/3 critical). 5/5 red lines held. Offered 10 conditional trade(s); recorded 0 concession/acceptance(s). Satisfaction gauge: 0.34.

Goals (7)
· untouched critical Secure a verifiable, written extension of the April 8 ceasefire to a permanent halt of US and Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear, leadership, and critical-infrastructure targets; ceasefire must include explicit Israeli adherence (currently absent from the bilateral track).
⏸ tabled critical Obtain reversal of the Sept 29, 2025 EU/UN snapback sanctions reimposition (Council Decision 2025/1971-1982) and de-listing of CBI, NIOC, and major Iranian banks from OFAC SDN lists; restore SWIFT messaging access; release of frozen FX reserves (estimated $100-120B).
· untouched critical Obtain implicit US recognition of the post-Khamenei leadership succession (Mojtaba Khamenei as Supreme Leader, Larijani as effective head of government) by entering binding agreements with their representatives — counters the Trump 'regime change' rhetoric of January 2026.
↑ advanced important Conditional offer: cap enrichment at 3.67% U-235 and submit any remaining 60% stockpile (post-strike inventory uncertain per IAEA) to chain-of-custody export to Russia, in exchange for full snapback reversal and lifting of secondary sanctions on Chinese oil offtake.
↑ advanced important Use the Hormuz closure as the principal piece of leverage: full IRGC Navy stand-down and reopening of the strait conditional on the above, with phased verifiable withdrawal of the US Navy blockade line declared during the April 13-18 enforcement window.
⏸ tabled important Restore IAEA inspections under Comprehensive Safeguards (Additional Protocol acceptance contingent on damage assessment of Fordow/Natanz/Isfahan); IAEA has been unable to inspect the attacked facilities since June 2025 and again since Feb 28, 2026.
· untouched nice_to_have Confidence-building measure: return of any Iranian-held US aircrew or personnel from the conflict; parallel humanitarian track on dual-national detainees and the post-strike civilian casualty registry.
Tactics observed from opponent
tac-stockpile-export-offer ×5 tac-hormuz-leverage ×10 tac-internal-ambiguity ×5 red-line-signaled ×2 verification-demand ×2 conditional-offer ×1 anchoring ×2 counter-anchoring ×5 deadline-pressure ×4

US
4.4/10
self-rating
Satisfaction +0.01 Trades offered 8 Agreements won 0 Red lines crossed against us 2

diplomacy-team-us concluded without formal agreement after 10/10 rounds. Advanced 3/7 goals (2/4 critical). 4/5 red lines held. Offered 8 conditional trade(s); recorded 2 concession/acceptance(s). Satisfaction gauge: 0.01.

Goals (7)
↑ advanced critical Achieve verified elimination of all Iranian 60%-enriched uranium — preferably via export to Russia for downblending and fabrication, with chain-of-custody and IAEA seals; the post-Feb 28 strike inventory is uncertain and resolving it is the central non-proliferation deliverable.
⏸ tabled critical Full IAEA Additional Protocol restoration with reinstated cameras at Natanz, Fordow, and Isfahan; resolution of long-standing access disputes at Turquzabad, Marivan, Varamin; bomb-damage assessment access to all sites struck June 22, 2025 (Operation Midnight Hammer) and Feb 28, 2026.
↑ advanced critical Complete and demonstrated Iranian stand-down at the Strait of Hormuz: uninterrupted commercial transit for 30 consecutive days, withdrawal of IRGC Navy checkpoint behavior (13 of 14 vessels turned back April 19 must reverse), transparent rules-of-engagement for all maritime forces.
⏸ tabled critical Preserve the operative effect of the Sept 29, 2025 EU/UN snapback (Council Decisions 2025/1971-1982) as enforcement leverage: any relief must be partial, time-bounded, and reversible without new UNSC action; the underlying authority remains with E3 and the US.
↑ advanced important Sanctions relief sequenced step-for-step against verified Iranian compliance — prefer SRE-style waivers over de-listings; CBI may be considered for limited humanitarian channeling; NIOC and IRGC-affiliated entities remain SDN.
· untouched important Maintain explicit alignment with Israel (which is not at the table but continues independent operations), the E3 (which triggered snapback Aug 28, 2025), and Gulf partners (Saudi/UAE/Bahrain/Kuwait — Iranian retaliatory missiles hit oil and gas facilities in their territory).
· untouched nice_to_have Confidence-building measure: return of any US aircrew or personnel held by Iran (note the F-15E rescue operation referenced in April 6, 2026 reporting), plus dual-national detainees on a parallel humanitarian track.
Tactics observed from opponent
tac-military-credibility ×4 tac-step-for-step ×10 tac-allied-consultation-frame ×7 tac-economic-pain-frame ×1 tac-hormuz-de-link ×1 verification-demand ×10 deadline-pressure ×3 counter-anchoring ×2
